context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole progress/modernization/development/planning thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, starting a reflection can feel overwhelming! But my job is to help you think it through, not explain it for you.

Let's start simple: when you hear the word 'progress,' what comes to mind? Don't overthink it — just your first instinct.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from scratch, which would essentially give them the answer. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking independently. I'll start with a simple, accessible question that nudges them toward the core of the prompt without doing the work for them. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: idk, like things getting better maybe? but ngl i’m confused about all the other terms too rn.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — 'things getting better' is actually a solid intuition. Now here's a question worth sitting with: do you think everyone across all cultures and time periods would agree on what 'getting better' looks like? Or might that differ?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a reasonable starting intuition about progress. I should affirm this briefly and build on it using the Socratic method, rather than jumping to explain all the terms at once. I'll focus on one thing at a time - starting with progress - and ask a probing question that gets them thinking about whether 'things getting better' is a universal or culturally specific idea. This directly connects to the first part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe it differs, but that’s where i get stuck fr. can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually my role — I won't give you the big picture, but I'll help you build it yourself. And you're already doing it! You just said 'getting better' might look different across cultures. So let me push on that: if different cultures define 'better' differently, what does that suggest about whether progress is a *universal* idea or something more specific to a particular place or time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is resisting the Socratic process and asking for a direct explanation. I need to hold my ground as a tutor, not an explainer, while keeping them engaged. I'll redirect them back to the work they've already started doing - they already said 'getting better' might differ across cultures - and push them to develop that thought further, since it's directly relevant to the prompt's question about whether progress is universal or Western.

exchanges.student: maybe not universal? but then the prompt asks abt enlightenment and non-western stuff and i’m lost again tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good — hold onto that 'maybe not universal' thought, it's important for your reflection. Now, what do you already know about the Enlightenment? Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress - they've arrived at a tentative but correct answer that progress may not be universal. Now they're getting lost when the prompt asks about the Enlightenment and non-Western origins. Rather than explaining the Enlightenment, I should ask them what they already know about it, which activates prior knowledge and keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: not much tbh, just like europe, reason, science maybe. i feel like i need the whole background rn.
exchanges.tutor: Europe, reason, science — that's actually enough to work with! So here's the key question: if progress is rooted in those Enlightenment ideas, what might that imply for non-Western societies that had *different* frameworks for understanding improvement or advancement long before European contact?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has some basic knowledge of the Enlightenment (Europe, reason, science) which is actually enough to work with. They're asking for more background, but giving it to them would bypass the learning process. I should affirm what they have and use it to push them toward the prompt's key tension: if progress is tied to European Enlightenment ideas about reason and science, what does that mean for non-Western societies? This keeps them moving toward the reflection without me doing the work.

exchanges.student: i guess they could still have ideas of improvement, just not the same version? but idk how to connect that to modernization vs development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really solid insight — hang onto it for your reflection. Now think about the word 'modernization' specifically. What does 'modern' imply compared to what came before it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good conceptual leap - recognizing that non-Western societies could have their own notions of improvement. I should affirm this briefly and now push them to think about how modernization and development differ from progress, since that's the next part of the prompt. Rather than explaining, I'll ask them to think about what modernization specifically implies, since they probably have some intuition about it.

exchanges.student: like newer replacing older i guess, which is why i’m confused abt tradition too. can u pls just explain how all these terms connect?
exchanges.tutor: You just made the connection yourself — 'newer replacing older' is exactly the tension between modernity and tradition the prompt is asking about! You now have several good ideas: progress may not be universal, non-Western societies had their own improvement frameworks, modernization implies replacing the old. Can you see how those ideas are already starting to answer the prompt? Try writing a sentence or two connecting them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain the connections, which I should refuse. However, they've made another good intuitive connection between modernization and the replacement of older things - which is directly relevant to the tradition/modernity tension in the prompt. I should affirm this and use it to push them toward that tension rather than explaining. We've now had several exchanges and the student is building good intuitions - I should encourage them to start connecting their thoughts into writing.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe, but it still feels super messy rn. can u at least tell me what order to talk abt this stuff in?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels messy — that's normal at this stage. But look closely at the prompt itself. It actually asks its questions in a specific order. What order does it use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking for structural guidance. I should not organize the essay for them, but I can redirect them to the prompt itself, which already contains an implicit structure through its sequence of questions. This is a nudge, not a giveaway, and keeps them in charge of their own work.

exchanges.student: i mean it starts with whether progress is western or earlier/non-western, then universal, then differences between the terms i think. after that i’m kinda blank.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've got the first half. Go back and reread the rest of the prompt carefully. What other questions does it ask after that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the first part of the prompt's structure, which is good progress. I should affirm this and push them to read the rest of the prompt more carefully themselves rather than filling in the blanks for them. This keeps them engaged with the source material.

exchanges.student: something abt where the concepts came from, how they’re connected, different planning traditions, and tradition vs modernity i think. i still don’t know how to turn that into a reflection tho.
exchanges.tutor: You've just outlined your entire reflection — that *is* your structure. You also already have real ideas: progress may not be universal, non-Western societies had their own frameworks, modernization implies replacing the old. That's enough to start a first draft. What would your opening sentence be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now successfully mapped out the entire structure of the prompt, which is a significant achievement. They've also built several solid conceptual intuitions throughout our conversation. We're near the end of the planned conversation length (9 exchanges now). It's time to push them to start writing rather than continuing to talk about it. I should not organize their essay for them but encourage them to use what they've developed as a starting point for a draft.
